## Key Success Metrics for Legal Tech

To measure the success of legal technology implementations, focus on three core areas: financial savings, work quality improvements, and user adoption. Tracking these key metrics ensures organizations get measurable returns on their investments.

### Financial Savings

Measuring cost efficiency is one of the most direct ways to evaluate legal tech ROI. According to [Statista](https://www.statista.com/statistics/1155993/legal-tech-return-on-investment-by-region-worldwide/), every dollar invested in legal tech in North America yielded an estimated $4.61 return on investment in 2021. | Outside Counsel Spend | 25-30% reduction |
| Cost-per-Matter Efficiency | 33% improvement |

## How to Calculate Legal Tech Returns

Measuring the return on investment (ROI) of legal tech requires a combination of financial analysis and operational insights. Without a structured measurement framework, firms may struggle to prove the real impact of their technology investments.

But how do you determine whether your legal tech is truly paying off? Imagine implementing a contract management system to streamline workflows, only to find that contract processing times remain unchanged. Without proper tracking, itâs difficult to pinpoint whether the issue lies in adoption, integration, or the tool itself.

A structured ROI calculation helps avoid these uncertainties by assessing measurable improvements, such as reduced contract review time, lower reliance on outside counsel, and increased billing accuracy.

### Establishing a Baseline

Before calculating ROI, it's essential to set clear benchmarks by documenting current costs, time commitments, and process inefficiencies. These baselines act as the starting point for measuring improvements after implementation.

Unlocking Success: A Guide to Legal Department KPIs & Metrics.

Natasha Norton

[](mailto:?body=https://chambers.com/articles/unlocking-success-a-guide-to-legal-department-kpis-metrics "Share this via Email")

ARTICLE 29 February 2024

In today's fast-paced and complex business environment, the role of legal departments within organisations has evolved significantly. Legal teams are no longer seen as mere support functions but are recognised as strategic partners contributing to the achievement of business objectives. However, to effectively fulfill this role, legal departments must demonstrate their value through measurable outcomes. This is where Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) and metrics play a crucial role.

### The Importance of Measuring Performance in Legal Departments

Measuring the performance of legal departments is essential for several reasons. Firstly, it allows organisations to assess the efficiency and effectiveness of legal operations, ensuring that resources are allocated optimally. Secondly, it provides insights into compliance and risk management, helping mitigate legal exposure and safeguarding the organisation's reputation. Lastly, measuring performance enables legal departments to enhance client satisfaction by delivering timely and quality legal services.

### An Overview of Key Performance Indicators and Metrics in the Legal Field

KPIs and metrics in the legal field are quantifiable measures used to evaluate various aspects of legal department performance. These measures encompass efficiency, compliance, risk management, and client satisfaction, among others. By tracking and analysing these metrics, legal departments can identify areas for improvement and make informed decisions to drive organisational success.

#### Understanding Law KPIs and Metrics

KPIs and metrics are tools used to quantify and evaluate performance against predefined objectives and targets. In the legal context, these indicators provide actionable insights into key aspects of legal department operations, facilitating informed decision-making and performance improvement initiatives.

#### _The Distinction Between Leading and Lagging Indicators_

Leading indicators are predictive measures that provide early warnings of potential issues or opportunities, allowing proactive intervention. Lagging indicators, on the other hand, are retrospective measures that assess past performance and outcomes. A balanced approach to incorporating both types of indicators is crucial for comprehensive performance evaluation in legal departments.

#### _Commonly Used KPIs and Metrics in Legal Departments_

1.   Efficiency Metrics
2.   Compliance Metrics
3.   Risk Management Metrics
4.   Client Satisfaction Metrics

### Key Metrics for Legal Department Performance Evaluation

#### _Efficiency Metrics_

Efficiency metrics focus on optimising resource utilisation and streamlining processes within the legal department. By tracking these metrics, organisations can identify areas for improvement and implement strategies to enhance efficiency and productivity.

*   Cost per Matter:Cost per matter is a fundamental efficiency metric that evaluates the cost-effectiveness of legal services. By calculating the total cost incurred for handling a specific matter or case, organisations can assess whether resources are being utilised efficiently and identify opportunities for cost reduction.
*   Time to Resolution:Time to resolution measures the average duration taken to resolve legal matters, including litigation, contracts, and regulatory issues. This metric provides insights into the speed of case resolution and the efficiency of legal processes. By reducing the time to resolution, organisations can minimise legal costs, mitigate risks, and enhance stakeholder satisfaction.
*   Staff Utilisation Rate:Staff utilisation rate measures the percentage of time that legal professionals spend on billable activities relative to their total available working hours. This metric helps organisations evaluate the efficiency of resource allocation and workload management within the legal department. By optimising staff utilisation, organisations can maximise productivity, improve service delivery, and enhance profitability.

#### _Compliance Metrics_

Compliance metrics are essential for ensuring that organisations adhere to legal and regulatory requirements. By monitoring these metrics, legal departments can identify compliance gaps, mitigate risks, and demonstrate regulatory adherence to stakeholders.

*   Regulatory Compliance Rate:Regulatory compliance rate measures the extent to which an organisation complies with applicable laws, regulations, and industry standards. This metric provides insights into the organisation's overall compliance posture and helps identify areas of non-compliance that require remediation. By maintaining a high regulatory compliance rate, organisations can mitigate legal risks, avoid penalties, and safeguard their reputation.
*   Audit Findings:Audit findings refer to the outcomes of internal or external audits conducted to assess the organisation's compliance with legal, regulatory, and internal control requirements. These findings may include instances of non-compliance, control deficiencies, or areas for improvement. By analysing audit findings, organisations can address compliance gaps, strengthen internal controls, and enhance overall governance and risk management practices.
*   Contract Review and Approval Cycle Time:Contract review and approval cycle time measures the average duration taken to review and approve contracts within the legal department. This metric evaluates the efficiency of contract management processes and identifies bottlenecks that may delay contract execution. By streamlining contract review and approval processes, organisations can accelerate deal closures, minimise legal exposure, and enhance stakeholder satisfaction.

#### _Risk Management Metrics:_

Risk management metrics help organisations identify, assess, and mitigate legal and operational risks. By monitoring these metrics, legal departments can proactively manage risks, protect organisational assets, and preserve shareholder value.

*   Litigation Risk Exposure:Litigation risk exposure assesses the potential legal risks faced by an organisation, including the likelihood of lawsuits, regulatory actions, and disputes. This metric helps organisations quantify and prioritise legal risks based on their severity and impact. By identifying potential litigation risks early, organisations can implement risk mitigation strategies, such as compliance programs, insurance coverage, and alternative dispute resolution mechanisms.
*   Claims Management Effectiveness:Claims management effectiveness measures the efficiency and effectiveness of handling legal claims and disputes within the organisation. This metric evaluates key performance indicators such as claims resolution time, settlement rates, and litigation costs. By optimising claims management processes, organisations can minimize financial losses, mitigate reputational risks, and enhance stakeholder trust and confidence.
*   Contractual Risk Assessment:Contractual risk assessment evaluates the level of risk associated with contractual agreements entered into by the organisation. This metric helps organisations identify contractual provisions that may expose them to legal liabilities, financial losses, or operational disruptions. By conducting comprehensive contractual risk assessments, organisations can negotiate favorable terms, mitigate contractual risks, and protect their interests in commercial transactions.

#### _Client Satisfaction Metrics_

Client satisfaction metrics measure the quality of legal services provided to internal and external clients. By monitoring these metrics, legal departments can identify areas for service improvement, enhance client relationships, and drive long-term business success.

*   Feedback Surveys:Feedback surveys gather input from clients regarding their satisfaction with legal services, responsiveness, communication, and overall experience. These surveys may be conducted periodically or after the completion of specific legal matters. By analysing client feedback, legal departments can identify strengths and weaknesses, address client concerns, and enhance service delivery to meet client expectations effectively.
*   Responsiveness and Resolution Time:Responsiveness and resolution time measure the speed and effectiveness of response to client inquiries, requests, and legal issues. This metric evaluates key performance indicators such as response times, resolution rates, and client satisfaction scores. By prioritising timely communication and efficient issue resolution, legal departments can enhance client satisfaction, build trust, and foster long-term client relationships.
*   Client Retention Rate:Client retention rate measures the percentage of clients who continue to engage the services of the legal department over time. This metric reflects the level of client loyalty, satisfaction, and perceived value derived from legal services. By maintaining a high client retention rate, legal departments can demonstrate their ability to meet client needs, deliver exceptional service, and foster positive client relationships that contribute to business growth and profitability.

### Establishing Effective KPIs and Metrics in Legal Departments

#### _Setting Strategic Objectives and Aligning KPIs with Organisational Goals_

To establish effective KPIs and metrics in legal departments, organisations must first define clear strategic objectives, aligned with overall organisational goals and priorities. By aligning KPIs with strategic objectives, legal departments can focus on measuring outcomes that directly contribute to the organisation's success and value creation.

#### _Defining Measurable and Actionable Metrics_

When defining KPIs and metrics, it is essential to ensure that they are specific, measurable, attainable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART). By defining clear and actionable metrics, legal departments can track progress, identify performance gaps, and implement targeted improvement initiatives effectively.

#### _Establishing Data Collection and Reporting Processes_

Effective data collection and reporting processes are essential for capturing accurate and reliable performance data for KPI measurement and analysis. Organisations must establish robust data collection mechanisms, leverage technology solutions, and define standardised reporting formats to ensure consistency, transparency, and accountability in performance reporting.

#### _Ensuring Data Accuracy and Reliability_

Data accuracy and reliability are critical considerations when measuring performance in legal departments. Organisations must implement quality assurance measures, validate data sources, and establish data governance frameworks to maintain data integrity and reliability. By ensuring data accuracy, legal departments can make informed decisions and drive continuous improvement based on reliable performance insights.

#### _Reviewing and Adapting KPIs and Metrics Over Time_

Continuous review and adaptation of KPIs and metrics are essential to ensure their relevance and effectiveness in measuring legal department performance. Organisations must periodically assess changing business needs, market dynamics, and regulatory requirements to refine existing KPIs, introduce new metrics, or retire obsolete indicators. By reviewing and adapting KPIs over time, legal departments can stay agile, responsive, and aligned with evolving organisational priorities.

### Implementing KPIs and Metrics in Legal Departments

#### _Building a Culture of Performance Measurement and Accountability_

Building a culture of performance measurement and accountability is essential for successful implementation of KPIs and metrics in legal departments. Organisations must foster a culture that values transparency, data-driven decision-making, and continuous improvement. By promoting accountability at all levels, legal departments can empower employees, align efforts with organisational goals, and drive performance excellence.

#### _Engaging Stakeholders and Obtaining Buy-In_

Engaging stakeholders and obtaining buy-in is crucial for garnering support and commitment for KPIs and metrics implementation in legal departments. Organisations must involve key stakeholders, including senior management, legal professionals, and cross-functional teams, in the development, implementation, and communication of performance measures. By fostering collaboration and communication, legal departments can ensure alignment with stakeholder expectations and enhance organisational effectiveness.

#### _Selecting and Implementing Appropriate Technology Solutions_

Selecting and implementing appropriate technology solutions are essential for enabling efficient data collection, analysis, and reporting of KPIs and metrics in legal departments. Organisations must leverage technology platforms, such as legal management systems, business intelligence tools, and dashboards, to automate data workflows, visualise performance metrics, and facilitate real-time decision-making. By embracing technology solutions, legal departments can enhance operational efficiency, productivity, and agility in responding to changing business needs.

#### _Training and Educating Legal Department Staff on KPIs and Metrics_

Training and educating legal department staff on KPIs and metrics are critical for fostering a culture of performance excellence and driving continuous improvement. Organisations must provide comprehensive training programs, workshops, and resources to familiarise legal professionals with performance measurement concepts, data collection methods, and analytical tools. By investing in staff training and development, legal departments can enhance employee competencies, engagement, and effectiveness in achieving organisational goals.

### Examples of how Legal KPIs and Metrics can be implemented.

#### _Example 1: Improving Cost Efficiency Through Effective Metrics Tracking_

Organisations can implement cost per matter metrics to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of legal services. By analysing cost data and identifying cost drivers, organisations may be able to identifiy opportunities to optimise resource allocation, negotiate favorable fee arrangements with external counsel, and implement alternative fee arrangements. This may enable organisations to achieve significant cost savings while maintaining service quality and legal outcomes.

#### _Example 2: Enhancing Compliance and Risk Management with Targeted KPIs_

Organisations can also implement regulatory compliance rate and litigation risk exposure metrics to strengthen compliance and risk management practices. By monitoring compliance levels and assessing litigation risks, organisations can identify areas for improvement, such as enhancing compliance training programs, implementing proactive risk mitigation strategies, and strengthening internal controls. As a result, organisations can reduce compliance-related incidents, mitigate legal risks, and enhance stakeholder confidence in their governance and risk management capabilities.

#### _Example 3: Enhancing Client Satisfaction and Service Delivery_

One final metric organisations can implement is client satisfaction surveys and responsiveness metrics to enhance client satisfaction and service delivery. By soliciting feedback from clients and measuring response times, organisations will be able to identifiy areas for service improvement, such as streamlining communication channels, enhancing legal advisory services, and providing timely updates on matter status. As a result, organisations can improve client satisfaction scores, strengthen client relationships, and gain competitive advantages in the marketplace.

In conclusion, the effective measurement and evaluation of performance through KPIs and metrics are essential for unlocking success in legal departments. By understanding key metrics, establishing effective measurement frameworks, and implementing targeted improvement initiatives, legal departments can enhance efficiency, ensure compliance, manage risks, and deliver superior client service. By fostering a culture of performance excellence and continuous improvement, legal departments can drive organisational success and contribute to long-term value creation.

In today's business environment, the ability to adapt and innovate is critical for legal departments to thrive. By embracing performance measurement and leveraging actionable insights from KPIs and metrics, legal departments can position themselves as strategic partners and value drivers within their organisations, driving sustainable growth and competitive advantage in an increasingly complex and challenging landscape.

You know how to practice law, but is a lack of direction impeding your ability to track the progress and growth of your firm? Success can feel elusive without a strategy to get you there, and the competition certainly isn't relying on intuition alone. That's where law firm key performance indicators (KPIs) come into play. When used strategically, KPIs provide the [data-driven insights](https://www.lawpay.com/about/blog/leverage-legal-analytics/) you need to achieve your firm's goals and stay ahead of the competition.

Tracking key metrics ensure that your team's work achieves meaningful, consistent results.

Whether you're starting a new venture or looking to bring your existing practice to new heights, KPIs are the ultimate tool for navigating toward success. Tracking key metrics ensures that your team's work achieves meaningful, consistent results.

However, with thousands of KPI metrics available, it's easy to feel overwhelmed. That's why we've compiled a list of the six essential KPIs that every law firm should track. With these metrics, you can confidently make informed decisions to support your firm’s vision for the future.

### Why is it important to track law firm KPIs?

Tracking the right metrics is essential for understanding the overall health of your firm and making data-driven decisions. KPIs provide measurable and actionable insights into the firm's performance and help you navigate the growth of your practice. Without them, it can be difficult to determine the effectiveness of your strategies and initiatives, thus leaving you vulnerable to undesirable results.

In short, KPIs help keep your law firm on track and moving in the right direction. They also help you stay ahead of potential problems and make strategic decisions to address them before they become significant issues.

Let's look at the three main reasons for tracking KPIs at your firm.

### Monitor Performance

Monitoring KPIs provides a clear picture of how your law firm is performing. It enables you to evaluate performance against specific metrics, giving you a better understanding of how your firm is doing relative to your goals. By analyzing your KPIs, you can identify trends and patterns that may be affecting your firm's performance and adjust your strategy accordingly.

### Maintain Accountability

Tracking KPIs helps to hold your law firm accountable for its goals. By setting measurable targets and regularly monitoring progress, you can ensure that your firm is making progress toward its objectives. Accountability also creates a sense of ownership and responsibility within the firm, helping to foster a culture of achievement and continuous improvement.

### Eliminate Guesswork

Data-driven decisions are critical to the success of all law firms. By tracking KPIs, you eliminate guesswork and make informed decisions based on hard data. Legal KPIs provide a clear and concise way to measure and evaluate your firm's progress, enabling you to identify areas where you need to improve and make the necessary adjustments to achieve your goals.

How To Choose Which KPIs To Measure
-----------------------------------

![Image 7: LawPay:LP-Blog-MeasureSuccess-600x200-BodyImageCallout-2](https://cdn-assets-us.frontify.com/s3/frontify-enterprise-files-us/eyJvYXV0aCI6eyJjbGllbnRfaWQiOiJjb250ZW50ZnVsIn0sInBhdGgiOiJhZmZpbmlwYXlcL2ZpbGVcL3lQa3BUVm5QbUtpa0NWUWJTRTJZLnBuZyJ9:affinipay:Oh0NmMmbJVk1H2XsMPENbF97hPjhUbZX48I2fYN7MKw)

There are thousands of data points you can measure when assessing your business. However, trying to quantify every aspect of your firm will likely lead to [frustration and burnout](https://www.lawpay.com/about/blog/prevent-lawyer-burnout/). Instead, focus your efforts where it matters most.

To help you determine which KPIs to track your firm, consider the following factors:

1.   **List Your Objectives.** What is it that you want to achieve in the short and long term? Create a list of realistic goals for your firm by assessing whether or not the achievements are [SMART](https://www.lawpay.com/about/blog/goals-for-lawyers/) (specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound). Knowing where you hope to end up provides valuable insight into your business and what data is needed to properly strategize toward success.

2.   **Evaluate Your Firm's Performance.** Take a closer look at your firm's current performance. Are there areas that need improvement? Are there opportunities for growth? By identifying areas that require attention, you can choose KPIs that will help measure and improve performance.

3.   **Determine What Data You Have.** Consider the [data and legal analytics](https://www.lawpay.com/about/blog/legal-analytics/) your firm currently has available. Do you have the necessary information to measure the KPIs you want to track? If not, determine how you can collect the data needed to measure your selected KPIs.

Many times, software and applications, like Google, most social media accounts, and your website host, automatically provide easy-to-read analytics. If you are using payment and billing software, like LawPay, you should already have critical data about your finances, staff productivity, and customer satisfaction at your fingertips.

1.   **Choose a Mix of Financial and Non-Financial KPIs.** Certain KPIs, such as your [law firm’s profitability](https://www.lawpay.com/about/blog/law-firm-profitability/)and revenue, are essential to measuring financial health. However, non-financial KPIs, such as client satisfaction and employee retention, can also provide valuable insights into your firm's performance. By choosing a blend of both types of metrics, you’ll create a comprehensive picture that tells a full story.

2.   **Keep It Simple.** While it's essential to measure a range of KPIs, it's also important to keep it simple. Choose a manageable number of KPIs to track that provide meaningful insights into your firm's performance.

---------------------------------------

While each law firm should consider its individual needs and objectives when selecting KPIs to track, there are six essential metrics that should be a part of every firm's performance evaluation strategy.

Monitor these client-related metrics to establish trust, boost client satisfaction, and uphold your firm's reputation:

## Key takeaways

## Why are legal metrics and KPIs so important for in-house teams now?

For many legal professionals, data-driven decision-making does not come naturally. The practice of law has long been driven by expertise, judgment, and precedent rather than numbers. However, as legal departments evolve, there is a growing expectation that they operate with the same level of efficiency, accountability, and strategic insight as other business units. Senior executives increasingly look to their legal teams to mitigate risk and contribute to business growth and operational success.

One of the biggest challenges legal teams face in this shift is understanding how to use data effectively. Many legal departments track various activities, but not all of them use that data in a way that aligns with business objectives. This is where the distinction between legal metrics and key performance indicators (KPIs) becomes crucial. While both are essential tools, they serve different purposes. Legal metrics offer a snapshot of legal department activities, while KPIs translate those activities into meaningful measures of success.

## What are legal metrics?Â

Legal metrics refer to raw, quantifiable data points that track specific legal activities or processes. They provide an objective measurement of what is happening within a legal department on a daily, weekly, or monthly basis. These metrics often focus on efficiency and volume, helping legal teams understand their workload, resource allocation, and operational effectiveness.

There are two types of legal metrics: **leading indicators** and **lagging indicators**. Leading indicators help predict future performance by identifying trends and potential bottlenecks early. For example, an increase in the number of contracts awaiting review could indicate an impending backlog. Lagging indicators, on the other hand, reflect past performance, such as the average time taken to close a contract or the total legal spend over a quarter. While lagging indicators are useful for assessing results, leading indicators can help legal teams proactively address challenges before they become major issues.

### Why legal metrics matter

## What are legal department KPIs?

Legal KPIs are a subset of metrics strategically chosen to measure progress toward specific legal department goals. While some legal metrics wonât correlate to business strategy, KPIs are tied directly to business objectives and provide meaningful insights into how legal operations contribute to overall company success.

A key characteristic of legal KPIs is that they are tied to specific **targets** and **timeframes**. For example, rather than simply tracking the number of contracts reviewed (a metric), a legal department might set a KPI to reduce contract cycle time by 20% over the next 12 months. This aligns with business goals such as improving deal velocity and accelerating revenue generation.

### The purpose of legal KPIs

Legal KPIs serve a more strategic function than general legal metrics. They help legal teams:

### Quantitative vs. qualitative KPIs

While many, even most, KPIs are quantitative, measuring aspects like cost savings and process efficiency, others are qualitative, focusing on the impact of legal services on business stakeholders. For example, **internal client feedback** can reflect how well the legal team supports internal business units. If the feedback is positive, it can demonstrate the teamâs value to senior leadership in a more powerful way than raw numbers can. If itâs negative, the team knows they have room to improve their reputation within the company, which can be vital for securing future resources and getting a seat at the table during major company decisions.

## Key differences between metrics and KPIs

Understanding the differences between legal metrics and KPIs is essential for using them effectively. While both involve tracking data, they serve distinct functions within a legal department's performance strategy:

To make this distinction more tangible, consider a personal example. If you track how much money you spend on groceries each month, that's a metric. If you set a monthly grocery budget of $500 and monitor whether you're staying under it, that's a KPI. The metric provides the data; the KPI tells you whether you achieve your goal.

## Why in-house legal teams need both

Legal departments cannot rely solely on either metrics or KPIsâboth are necessary for a comprehensive data strategy. Metrics provide the operational detail and context, while KPIs align those activities with strategic business goals.

Metrics are essential for understanding the current state of legal operations. They highlight opportunities for improved workload, efficiency, and resource allocation. However, without KPIs, metrics lack the strategic framing necessary to demonstrate how legal work contributes to the companyâs success. On the other hand, KPIs offer a clear picture of progress toward goals, but without supporting metrics, it can be challenging to identify whatâs working or where improvement is needed.

For example, tracking the **number of contracts reviewed** (a metric) can help a legal team understand workload distribution. However, setting a KPI to **reduce contract cycle time by 20% over six months** ties that data to a business objective, such as accelerating deal velocity. The metric provides the raw data; the KPI provides direction.

## Choosing the right legal department KPIs

Using KPIs effectively also requires selecting the right ones. Choosing the wrong KPI can lead to unintended consequences. For instance, if a legal department chooses only one KPI, such as âtime to close a legal request,â they might meet that goal at the expense of other priorities. A team overly focused on speed could inadvertently damage relationships with internal clients by rushing through requests and missing essential details.

In such cases, a single KPI doesn't tell the whole story. That's why it's important to select a small set of complementary KPIs that balance efficiency with quality and client satisfaction. For example, pairing âtime to closeâ with a KPI measuring internal client satisfaction ensures that efficiency gains donât come at the cost of service quality.

The takeaway: metrics and KPIs should work together, and the right mix of KPIs should reflect a balanced view of what success looks like across operational, strategic, and relationship-focused dimensions.

Implementing a system for tracking legal metrics and KPIs can feel overwhelming at first, especially for teams that havenât historically relied on data to guide operations. The transition to a more data-driven legal function requires a cultural shift as well as a practical one, but it doesnât have to happen all at once.

The first step is to help your team recognize the importance of consistency and accuracy in recording and using data. Inconsistent or incomplete data undermines the reliability of insights and hampers the legal teamâs ability to make informed decisions. A Strategic Guide for Legal Departments to Optimize Their Technology Stack and Drive Efficiency in 2025
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The start of a new year brings a fresh opportunity to take stock of what’s working and what’s not – especially when it comes to your legal tech stack. With technology playing a critical role in driving efficiency and cost savings, understanding how your tools are performing is essential.

_Are there gaps where processes are falling through? Are some solutions overlapping and creating redundancies? Or is there room for improvement that could unlock untapped potential?_ These are all questions you are probably asking.

By taking the time to evaluate your legal tech stack, you can identify inefficiencies, streamline workflows, and make smarter investments. But it’s not just about cutting costs; it’s about empowering your team with the right tools to work at their best.

Dive in to uncover 5 steps to take when beginning your legal tech evaluation process.

![Image 20: How Legal Matter Management Software Supports Regulatory Readiness](https://mitratech.com/wp-content/uploads/Mitratech-HRC_Blog-Dividers_Bug-12.png)

### Step 1: Assess Current Performance of Tech Stack

To begin evaluating your legal tech stack, it’s essential to evaluate how well your existing legal tech stack has performed over the past year (and beyond!). This step helps you understand what’s working, what’s not, and where adjustments are needed to enhance efficiency. It will also help ensure that your [legal operations team is aligned with company goals](https://clc.mitratech.com/speaking-the-language-of-your-legal-operations-stakeholders?utm_source=blog&utm_medium=digital-asset&utm_campaign=clc-evaluating-your-legal-tech-stack-feb-2025), addressing pain points effectively, and leveraging the right technology.

Start by analyzing the performance of your current tools against key metrics and benchmarks. Consider indicators such as:

*   **Time saved:** Have your tools streamlined processes and reduced manual work?
*   **Cost reductions:** Are the solutions delivering measurable value in terms of cost savings?
*   **User adoption rates:** Are your team members actively using the tools, or are certain systems underutilized due to complexity or lack of training?

[![Image 21: Legal Ops checklist](https://mitratech.com/wp-content/uploads/Legal-Ops-Checklist_Thumbnail-300x300.png)](https://clc.mitratech.com/speaking-the-language-of-your-legal-operations-stakeholders?utm_source=blog&utm_medium=digital-asset&utm_campaign=clc-evaluating-your-legal-tech-stack-feb-2025)

Gathering feedback is another important component of this evaluation. Engage with your legal team and stakeholders to gain insights into their experiences with the current tech stack.

 Consider questions such as:

*   Are there specific pain points or frustrations you’ve encountered?
*   Are certain tools falling short of expectations or causing inefficiencies?
*   On the flip side, are there standout tools that have delivered significant value?

This feedback, combined with performance metrics, provides a clear picture of where your tech stack is excelling and where it needs improvement. It lays the foundation for making informed decisions as you optimize your tools for the year ahead.

### Step 2: Identify Gaps and Pain Points in Existing Tech Stack

To optimize your legal tech stack, it’s crucial to identify inefficiencies and bottlenecks that hinder performance. A common issue is the fragmentation of technology solutions, where [disparate systems lead to disjointed processes](https://clc.mitratech.com/on-demand-webinar/dead-talk-workflow-automation?utm_source=blog&utm_medium=digital-asset&utm_campaign=clc-tap-evaluating-your-legal-tech-stack-feb-2025) and duplicated efforts. For example, do you have one solution for contract management and another for document generation, but still have to manually upload or input data to keep the process moving? This fragmentation can cause significant inefficiencies within legal teams.

When evaluating your legal tech stack, it is also important to understand and map out your workflows. Misaligned processes, (such as scattered client information across multiple platforms, for example), can lead to unclear communication and increase the likelihood of errors or lost data. By identifying these gaps, you can streamline workflows and maximize the value of your technology stack.

Common challenges include:

*   **Poor integrations:** Systems that don’t communicate effectively can create data silos, disrupt workflows, and force your team to rely on manual workarounds, leading to inefficiencies and potential errors.
*   **Outdated systems:** Legacy tools may lack the features or scalability required to meet current demands, slowing down processes and making it harder to stay competitive in a rapidly evolving legal landscape.

![Image 22: Evaluating Your Legal Tech Stack for the New Year](https://mitratech.com/wp-content/uploads/Mitratech-HRC_trackeri9_web-elements_healthcare-i-9-management_4_error-detection.png)

*   **Security concerns:** Unsupported or outdated software can leave your organization vulnerable to data breaches and compliance risks, especially as cybersecurity threats continue to rise.

By pinpointing these gaps and pain points, you can make informed decisions to enhance your tech stack, leading to improved performance and reduced operational friction.

### Step 3: Evaluate Legal Technology Vendor Relationships and Support

Your relationships with your legal tech vendors is just as important as the tools and services they provide. A strong technology partner should not only deliver effective solutions but also empower you to use those tools to their fullest potential. As the saying goes, “give a man a fish, and you feed him for a day; teach a man to fish, and you feed him for a lifetime.” Similarly, the right vendor will go beyond quick fixes, equipping your team with the knowledge, training, and support needed to achieve long-term success and self-sufficiency with their technology.

A thorough evaluation of these partnerships ensures that you’re getting the support, innovation, and value you need to meet your legal team’s goals.
